author: @kennybentley1161
text:
rob, I was thinking about it and my brother has an electric wheelchair but he doesn't use it because the thing weighs a ton and charging it all the time is a hassle (mostly the weight though). anyway, a lot of his friends have electric wheelchairs and then yesterday I realised that wheelchairs need replacement batteries all the time.


if you could make a wheelchair replacement battery which weighs less, and charges in a short period of time, you're totally gonna rock that market. they typically have 12v lead acid batteries, so it's essentially a replacement for a 12v car batterie essentially (another pretty big market which battery replacement doesn't void the warrantee)


having a battery that can charge rapidly is the real selling point because it really sucks to get stuck out some place with a dead battery that takes 6 hrs to charge


cheers

author: @xavierbacon7538
text:
Am I right in thinking you were a lecturer at Southampton Uni in a previous life?
Sometime in the 1990's I visited an exhibition stand (Electricity Council?) at NEC Electrex and met a Southampton Uni lecturer who was shepherding a very intense graduate in his attempts to launch his radical electric traction motor design. Basically it was a longitudinally squashed DC series motor of about 300mm OD, with a full sized faceplate commutator, that ran very efficiently at low speed with high torque. On demo was a very heavy 'butcher's bike', powered by this motor, that had run successfully from Southampton to Rugby (100 miles?) with just one charge on its 12 volt lead/acid car battery.
Often wondered what happened to what looked like a piece of revolutionary English design engineering. Any ideas about the project - were you involved with it?

author: @BurnabyAlex
text: Good use of using a pin through a hole and then wetted with more ink. I think you could  use that for the audio connector for your electrostatic speakers.      I wonder how good an antenna the ink would make for a DIY HDTV antenna.

text: Another good job, Rob!

author: @William_Hada
text: Guitar players shield the electronics inside the guitar body cavity by lining it with copper or aluminum foil. Painting the cavity with your ink would be much easier and cheaper to do than foil.

author: @ollieb9875
text: Robert! I put "some" magnesium powder and used potassium permanganate as a fuse, added glycerin and now my neighbour hates me for shaking the building and making a loud noise 🤣 think I used 2.5g of each... I was in the garden which is quite large (2 acre). How big is too big though.. for guy Fawkes .. 4g magnesium? Science is fun.  😁

replies.author: @ollieb9875
replies.text: Also sodium, it's asleep in paraffin at the moment but I don't trust it... I have too much of it.

author: @scotttovey
text:
Arduino has many inputs, so you can assign each piezo to a different drum in the kit. You could use a variable resister to do something like a slide trombone. 
You could use various lengths of resistance lines and program the Arduino to play either an electronic organ or piano note based on the amount of resistance each line has.

Lots of possibilities with this like paint a computer keyboard.
The keyboard may not be a good idea if you are heavy handed on a regular keyboard the way some people are. The lack of key movement in a painted keyboard would cause people to injure their fingers while hammering on it the way they do a regular keyboard.
